title: Predecessors
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$. The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ are: \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\] We also put: \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_9 -- "Predecessors".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_9
% title: Predecessors
\def\rowref{def\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_9}

\begin{Def}[Predecessors]
    \label{def-predecessors}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a \emph{strict} total order in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order} (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN -- in particular, the LN does \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ -- and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def \ref{not-cdmg}, item~1, that ``$w \in G$'' abbreviates ``$w \in J \cup V$'', we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

    The set of \emph{predecessors} of $v$ in $G$ is
    \[
        \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC.
    \]
    We also put
    \[
        \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.
    \]

    \emph{Notational quirk: the ``$\le$'' subscript vs.\ the strict body.} The subscript ``$\le$'' on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is \emph{strict} (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, \emph{not} the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

    \emph{Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$.} Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a strict total order in the sense of def def-topological-order (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN – in particular, the LN does not impose $v \in J \cup V$ – and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def not-cdmg, item 1, that “$w \in G$” abbreviates “$w \in J \cup V$”, we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC. \] We also put \[ \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}. \]

Notational quirk: the “$\le$” subscript vs.\ the strict body. The subscript “$\le$” on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is strict (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, not the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do not impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `Pred` and `PredLE`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the set-builder body `{w | w ∈ G ∧ lt w v}` invokes `w ∈ G`, which via the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` reduces to `Finset` membership on `G.J ∪ G.V` — and `Finset` operations require decidable equality on the element type. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not needed here and is left to downstream use sites that actually consume it.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `w ∈ G` via Finset membership.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This block formalises the strict predecessor set $\Pred^G_<(v) = \{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$. Under `def_3_2`'s `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, `w ∈ G` unfolds to `w ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, so the set-builder body reads literally as the LN's $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$. Four arguments: the graph `G`, the strict relation `lt`, an explicit hypothesis `(_h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` sitting between `lt` and `v`, and the target node `v`; output type `Set Node`. The hypothesis anchors the LN's *"let `<` be a total order of `J ∪ V`"* premise inside Lean's type contract — every caller must supply the witness, so `G.Pred lt h v` and $\Pred^G_<(v)$ coincide by construction. The leading underscore on `_h` is Lean's standard convention for an *explicitly bound but intentionally unused* binder: it silences the `unusedVariables` linter while keeping the parameter positional (callers pass the witness as the third argument regardless of binder name; `PredLE` does this with its own `h`). The body never inspects the witness — `h` is a phantom enforced at type-check time at every use site, plumbing rather than data.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: def_3_9 (strict predecessors) — type-level total-order witness threaded through the signature.
-- `Pred` is a top-level def under `namespace CDMG`, so callers write `G.Pred lt h v`.
def Pred (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node → Node → Prop)
    -- `_h` carries the total-order hypothesis explicitly (so callers must supply it)
    -- but is underscored to silence `unusedVariables` since the body never reads it.
    (_h : G.IsTotalOrder lt) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  -- Literal LN body `{w ∈ J ∪ V | w < v}`: `w ∈ G` routes through def_3_2 to Finset membership.
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ lt w v}

lean_blocks.explanation: This block formalises the non-strict predecessor set $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$ — the strict predecessor set adjoined with the node itself. The body reads on the nose as the LN's literal spelling: rather than unfolding to `{w | w ∈ G ∧ (lt w v ∨ w = v)}` or `{w | w ≤ v}`, it calls the named `G.Pred lt h v` and unions with the `Set Node` singleton `{v}` (`Set.instSingleton` auto-resolves because `∪`'s left operand is already a `Set Node`). The signature carries the same `(h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` hypothesis as `Pred`, but here the binder is `h` *without* an underscore: the body threads it into `G.Pred lt h v`, filling `Pred`'s `_h` slot positionally. That's the convention's other half — underscore means "phantom", no underscore means "forwarded or consumed". The corner case `v ∉ G` is preserved: `v ∈ G.PredLE lt h v` *unconditionally*, even outside `J ∪ V`.

design_choices:
**Typing context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]`, inherited from `def_3_1`.** The decidability instance is load-bearing for this row: the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` (`CDMGNotation.lean`) unfolds `w ∈ G` to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`, which routes through `DecidableEq Node`. Stronger structure on `Node` (`Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to use sites that actually consume it. The `variable` line carries the three-dash `--- start helper` marker rather than the two-dash `-- start statement` flavour: the latter is reserved for declarations whose body is the formalised LN content of the row, while this binding is statement-typing infrastructure — it supplies the implicit parameters `Pred` / `PredLE` reference but is not itself part of the LN definition. Same convention as the rest of the section's files.

**`lt : Node → Node → Prop` is an explicit external argument, not a `[LT Node]` typeclass or a bundled structure field.** The LN's subscript $<$ in $\Pred^G_<(v)$ is a *parameter* the predecessor set is computed against, not a structural feature of nodes. A `[LT Node]` instance would glue a single canonical strict order to the *type* `Node`, but one `Node` type underlies every CDMG and each graph admits many topological orders — there is no canonical pick. A bundled `structure TopologicalOrder G where lt + props` would conflate the parameter convention with `claim_3_2`'s existence claim and force every consumer who just wants to *use* a chosen `lt` to package it first. Keeping `lt` raw and adding the order property as a separate `Prop`-valued hypothesis matches the parameter convention `def_3_8`'s `IsTopologicalOrder` already uses — see the full rejection of typeclass / structure encodings in the design block in `TopologicalOrder.lean`.

**`Pred` and `PredLE` take `(h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` as an explicit positional hypothesis between `lt` and `v`, anchoring the LN's *"let `<` be a total order of $J \cup V$"* premise in Lean's type contract.** $\Pred^G_<(v)$ is only well-defined when $<$ is a total order on $J \cup V$ (the LN block opens exactly that way), so the signature requires the witness. Without it, `G.Pred lt v` would be well-typed for *any* binary relation `lt`, pushing the total-order premise off the type checker and into prose. Threading `h` through the signature anchors the LN premise at the type level: every downstream consumer must supply the total-order witness, so `G.Pred lt h v` and $\Pred^G_<(v)$ coincide *by construction*. The hypothesis is `Prop`-valued, not a typeclass `[G.IsTotalOrder lt]` or a structure-field encoding, matching `lt`'s own `Prop`-shape and avoiding bracket-threading at every use site.

**The helper is `IsTotalOrder`, not the stronger `IsTopologicalOrder` — even though every downstream consumer in chapters 4+ has the latter in scope.** The LN's premise *for this row* reads "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" — nothing more. The parent-precedence conjunct of `IsTopologicalOrder` ($\forall v\,w,\,v \in \Pa^G(w) \Rightarrow lt\,v\,w$) is not required for the predecessor-set computation $\{w \in G \mid lt\,w\,v\}$ to be well-defined: any total order on $J \cup V$ suffices. Demanding the full `IsTopologicalOrder` here would over-constrain the few legitimate consumers of non-topological total orders — chapter 5's ID-algorithm "preceding Markov blanket" slice (`id-algorithm.tex` §lines 227–240) carves $J \cup V$ into $\{w \mid w < v\}$, $\{v\}$, $\{w \mid v < w\}$ purely via the total-order content, and the factorisation reverse-ordering step (`id-algorithm.tex` line 466) reverses a total order qua total order. Consumers that *do* hold a `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` witness reach `IsTotalOrder` via the first projection `(h_topo : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt).1` — one step, no plumbing. This matches the helper-vs-full-predicate split spelled out in the `IsTotalOrder` block in `TopologicalOrder.lean`.

**`_h` vs `h` underscore convention: phantom in `Pred`'s body, threaded in `PredLE`'s.** `Pred`'s body — `{w | w ∈ G ∧ lt w v}` — never inspects the total-order witness; the hypothesis exists purely as a type-level contract whose role is to force the caller to supply a proof, not to feed the set-builder. The parameter is bound with the leading-underscore name `_h`, Lean's / Mathlib's standard convention for "explicitly bound but intentionally unused": this silences the `unusedVariables` linter while keeping the parameter positional, so callers pass the witness as the third positional argument regardless of binder name. `PredLE`'s body, by contrast, *does* reference its hypothesis — it threads `h` into the call `G.Pred lt h v`, filling `Pred`'s `_h` slot positionally — so there the binder is named `h` without the underscore. A reader scanning the two signatures can tell at a glance which body forwards the witness and which holds it inertly.

**Two top-level `def`s — strict `Pred` and non-strict `PredLE` — rather than a single operator parameterised on a `Bool` flag or a `notation` abbreviation.** The LN explicitly introduces *both* $\Pred^G_<$ and $\Pred^G_\le$, and both names recur under that distinction in later chapters: CBN factorisation (chapter 4) and do-calculus (chapter 5) reach for the strict form, while iSCM recursion (chapters 8–10, "values determined up to and including $v$") reaches for the non-strict one. Folding the choice into `def Pred (G) (lt) (h) (strict : Bool) (v)` would force every call site to thread a `Bool` and peel off a `match` before reasoning about set membership; a pure `notation` abbreviation would leave `PredLE` un-`@[simp]`-able as its own head symbol. Exposing two named `def`s avoids both — the second is a one-line wrapper `G.Pred lt h v ∪ {v}`, so the duplication cost is negligible. Both `def`s sit under `namespace CDMG`, so callers write the natural `G.Pred lt h v` / `G.PredLE lt h v` dot-form, follow Mathlib `PascalCase`, and the `LE` suffix in `PredLE` signals the non-strict variant.

**Output type `Set Node`, not `Finset Node` or the subtype `↥(G.J ∪ G.V) → Set ↥(G.J ∪ G.V)`.** The chapter's family-set primitives (`Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`, `Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist` from `def_3_5`) all live in `Set Node`, and downstream uses freely intersect predecessor sets with them (e.g.\ $\Pred^G_<(v) \cap \Pa^G(v)$ in do-calculus, $\Pred^G_<(v) \cap V$ in CBN factorisation). A `Finset Node` carrier would thread a `Decidable (lt w v)` instance through every call site — the LN never picks a decidable representative for $<$, and the total-order hypothesis `h` is a bare `Prop` that does not produce one — and would force `Finset.coe` round-trips against the ambient family sets. The subtype carrier would force `Subtype.val` coercions throughout and make `PredLE`'s adjoined `{v}` ill-typed in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. The `w ∈ G` conjunct in `Pred`'s body mirrors the LN's literal $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$ and makes the membership witness available immediately on destructuring `h_w : w ∈ G.Pred lt h v` as `⟨hw_mem, hw_lt⟩`.

**`PredLE`'s body is the literal LN spelling `G.Pred lt h v ∪ {v}`, not a reflexive-closure form `{w | w ∈ G ∧ (lt w v ∨ w = v)}` and not `insert v (G.Pred lt h v)`.** The LN subscript reads $\le$ but the body it writes is *strict* — $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\} \cup \{v\}$, with the singleton adjoined unconditionally. The two forms agree whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$) and diverge only in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$: under the literal LN body the adjoined `{v}` carries no `v ∈ G` guard, so `v ∈ G.PredLE lt h v` *unconditionally*. The total-order hypothesis `h` constrains `lt` on $J \cup V$, not the ambient `Node` type, so it leaves the set-theoretic semantics on nodes outside `G` untouched. The body is spelled with the named `G.Pred` rather than unfolded — it reads on the nose as "the strict predecessor set plus $v$", and downstream proofs holding `hw : w ∈ G.Pred lt h v` can apply `Or.inl hw` to obtain `w ∈ G.PredLE lt h v` with no intermediate `Set.mem_setOf` unfolding. The singleton `{v}` resolves to `Set.instSingleton` because Lean's elaboration sees `∪` on a `Set Node` left-hand side; `∪ {v}` is preferred over `insert v (G.Pred lt h v)` because it reads most directly as "predecessors plus the node itself", and `Set.mem_union` / `Set.mem_singleton_iff` are the natural destructors.
